section.title: Hezekiah Restores Worship
section.sectionRationale: After Passover, the assembly fans out across Judah and even into former Israelite territory, tearing down every remaining high place and altar. Hezekiah then reestablishes the priestly and Levitical divisions, and the people respond with such abundant tithes and firstfruits that storerooms must be built and dedicated officials appointed to distribute the surplus, leaving the chronicler to summarize simply that Hezekiah acted with all his heart and so he prospered.
section.thesis: The overflow of generous, willing giving that follows genuine worship shows that reform is not complete until it reaches the ordinary rhythms of tithe and provision, and that a heart wholly given to God in worship naturally produces open handed generosity toward His house and His servants.
section.frameNote: Three beats: the destruction of remaining idols across the land, verse 1; the reorganization of priestly and Levitical service and the people's overflowing contributions, verses 2 to 10; and Hezekiah's careful administration of the abundance and the chronicler's summary of his wholehearted devotion, verses 11 to 21.

verses.text: When all this had ended, the Israelites in attendance went out to the cities of Judah and broke up the sacred pillars, chopped down the Asherah poles, and tore down the high places and altars throughout Judah and Benjamin, as well as in Ephraim and Manasseh, until they had utterly destroyed them all. Then all the Israelites returned to their cities, each to his own property.

Hezekiah reestablished the divisions of the priests and Levites, each of them according to their duties as priests or Levites, for the burnt offerings and peace offerings, for ministry, for giving thanks, and for singing praises at the gates of the LORD's dwelling.

The king contributed from his own possessions for the regular morning and evening burnt offerings and for the burnt offerings on the Sabbaths, New Moons, and appointed feasts, as written in the Law of the LORD.
Moreover, he commanded the people living in Jerusalem to make a contribution for the priests and Levites so that they could devote themselves to the Law of the LORD.

As soon as the order went out, the Israelites generously provided the firstfruits of the grain, new wine, oil, and honey, and of all the produce of the field, and they brought in an abundance, a tithe of everything.

And the Israelites and Judahites who lived in the cities of Judah also brought a tithe of their herds and flocks and a {{maaser|tithe}} of the holy things consecrated to the LORD their God, and they laid them in large heaps.
In the third month they began building up the heaps, and they finished in the seventh month.
When Hezekiah and his officials came and viewed the heaps, they blessed the LORD and His people Israel.
Then Hezekiah questioned the priests and Levites about the heaps,

and Azariah, the chief priest of the household of Zadok, answered him, "Since the people began to bring their contributions into the house of the LORD, we have had enough to eat, and there is plenty left over, because the LORD has blessed His people; this great abundance is what is left over."
Then Hezekiah commanded them to prepare storerooms in the house of the LORD, and they did so.
And they faithfully brought in the contributions, tithes, and dedicated gifts. Conaniah the Levite was the officer in charge of them, and his brother Shimei was second.

Jehiel, Azaziah, Nahath, Asahel, Jerimoth, Jozabad, Eliel, Ismachiah, Mahath, and Benaiah were overseers under the authority of Conaniah and his brother Shimei, by appointment of King Hezekiah and of Azariah the chief official of the house of God.
Kore son of Imnah the Levite, the keeper of the East Gate, was in charge of the freewill offerings given to God, distributing the contributions to the LORD and the consecrated gifts.

Under his authority, Eden, Miniamin, Jeshua, Shemaiah, Amariah, and Shecaniah faithfully distributed portions to their fellow priests in their cities, according to their divisions, old and young alike.

In addition, they distributed portions to the males registered by genealogy who were three years of age or older, to all who would enter the house of the LORD for their daily duties for service in the responsibilities of their divisions,
and to the priests enrolled according to their families in the genealogy, as well as to the Levites twenty years of age or older, according to their responsibilities and divisions.
The genealogy included all the little ones, wives, sons, and daughters of the whole assembly. For they had faithfully consecrated themselves as holy.

As for the priests, the descendants of Aaron, who lived on the farmlands around each of their cities or in any other city, men were designated by name to distribute a portion to every male among the priests and to every Levite listed by the genealogies.
So this is what Hezekiah did throughout Judah. He did what was good and {{yashar|upright}} and true before the LORD his God.
He acted with all his {{lev|heart}} in every work that he began in the service of the house of God, and in the law and the commandments, in order to seek his God. And so he prospered.

maaser.gloss: Tithe, a tenth part. The people bring a maaser of their herds, flocks, and even the holy things already consecrated to the LORD, an abundance so great that new storerooms had to be built, showing that genuine worship overflows naturally into generous, practical provision.

yashar.gloss: Straight, right, or upright. Hezekiah did what was good and yashar, upright, and true before the LORD; the same root elsewhere describes a level, straight path, suggesting a reign without the crooked compromises that marked so many other kings.

lev.gloss: Heart. Hezekiah acted with all his lev, heart, in every work of the temple's service and the law, the chronicler's highest form of commendation, tying his prosperity directly to the wholeness of his devotion rather than to mere outward achievement.

basic.context: Once the Passover celebration ends, the assembled Israelites fan out across Judah and even into the former northern territory of Ephraim and Manasseh, tearing down every remaining high place, sacred pillar, and Asherah pole they can find. Hezekiah then turns to the practical, ongoing work of restoring temple worship, reestablishing the priestly and Levitical divisions and calling the people to bring their tithes and firstfruits so the priests and Levites can devote themselves fully to the Law. The people's response is so overwhelming that huge heaps of grain, wine, oil, and livestock accumulate over four months, requiring new storerooms and a careful system of distribution to be organized under trustworthy officials. The chronicler summarizes Hezekiah's whole approach in a single sentence: he did what was good and upright and true before the LORD, acting with all his heart, and so he prospered.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative describing the practical, sustaining infrastructure that genuine worship reform requires, not merely dramatic moments of destruction or celebration but the ongoing, unglamorous work of tithing, distribution, and provision for those who serve. It shows that true reform touches the economic and administrative life of a community, not only its ceremonies.
basic.originalLanguages: The people bring an overwhelming maaser, tithe, of their produce and livestock, so abundant that new storerooms are required. The chronicler's verdict on Hezekiah is that he did what was good and yashar, upright, and true. And the reason given for his prosperity is that he acted with all his lev, heart, in the temple's service and in seeking his God, tying material and spiritual prosperity directly to wholehearted devotion.
basic.typology: The overflowing tithes that require new storerooms to contain them, given freely once the people had truly encountered the LORD in worship, anticipate the New Testament pattern of generous, cheerful giving that flows naturally from hearts transformed by grace, foreshadowing the abundant, willing provision the early church models in Acts 2 and 4.
basic.christ: Hezekiah's wholehearted devotion, described as seeking his God with all his heart in the service of the house of God, models the kind of undivided love for God that the greatest commandment calls for, and that Christ alone perfectly fulfilled. The overflowing generosity of the people, once true worship was restored, foreshadows how encountering the grace of Christ produces open handed, joyful giving that no longer counts the cost, since Christ Himself, though rich, became poor for our sake, that we through His poverty might become rich, 2 Corinthians 8:9.
basic.god: God is shown here to bless material and practical abundance directly in response to sincere worship and generous obedience; the priest Azariah's own testimony, we have had enough to eat, and there is plenty left over, because the LORD has blessed His people, credits this abundance explicitly to God's own hand. God also delights in careful, faithful administration of what He provides, seen in the detailed, trustworthy systems Hezekiah establishes to ensure the gifts reach those who serve.
basic.crossReferences: Malachi 3:10 invites Israel to test God by bringing the full tithe, promising blessing poured out in overflowing measure, exactly the pattern seen here. Acts 2:44-45 and Acts 4:32-35 describe the early church's generous, voluntary sharing that flows from genuine encounter with the risen Christ. 2 Corinthians 9:6-8 teaches that God loves a cheerful giver and provides abundantly for every good work. Deuteronomy 6:5 commands loving the LORD with all your heart, the standard Hezekiah is commended for meeting.
basic.oneStory: The overflowing generosity that follows Hezekiah's restoration of true worship shows that genuine reform reaches beyond ceremony into the practical, generous provision of a community's daily life. Hezekiah's wholehearted devotion, seeking God with all his heart and prospering as a result, anticipates the perfect, undivided love for God fulfilled in Christ, whose grace produces in His people the same overflowing, joyful generosity once demonstrated at this restored temple in Jerusalem.

context.literary: The chapter moves from the sweeping destruction of idolatry across two former kingdoms' worth of territory, verse 1, into the detailed, almost administrative texture of tithes, storerooms, and distribution rosters, verses 2 to 19, before closing with the chronicler's compact theological summary of Hezekiah's entire approach, verses 20 to 21, a movement from dramatic reform to sustaining structure to summarizing verdict.
context.historical: The four month span, from the third month to the seventh month, during which the tithe heaps accumulated, corresponds to the harvest seasons of grain, grapes, and other produce throughout the agricultural year, reflecting a genuinely comprehensive, nationwide response rather than a single event.
context.canonical: Hezekiah's summary commendation, he did what was good and upright and true before the LORD his God, He acted with all his heart, stands among the strongest positive evaluations any king of Judah receives in Chronicles, anticipating the even fuller commendation later given to Josiah, that no king before or after him turned to the LORD as he did.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that Hezekiah's reform did not stop with ceremony but extended into the practical, sustained provision for temple worship, reestablishing priestly divisions and calling forth such generous tithing that the whole system had to be carefully reorganized to manage the abundance, all summarized as the fruit of a heart wholly given to seeking God.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter narrates specific historical arrangements for tithes and priestly divisions under the old covenant sacrificial system; it does not prescribe identical structures for the church today. What endures is the principle that genuine worship produces genuine, overflowing generosity, and that faithful administration of what is given matters as much as the giving itself.

debates.question: Why does the chronicler give such extensive, granular detail about the distribution system, naming so many individual officials?

views.label: Honoring faithful, often unseen administrative service
views.case: By naming each overseer and distributor in detail, the chronicler ensures that unglamorous, behind the scenes faithfulness, not just dramatic public reform, receives recognition as genuinely valuable service to God, modeling that every role in sustaining worship matters.

views.label: Demonstrating the thoroughness and integrity of the reform
views.case: The careful naming of officials responsible for such a large volume of goods serves as evidence of transparent, accountable administration, showing that Hezekiah's reform was not merely enthusiastic but also carefully, honestly managed to prevent corruption or waste.

debates.bearing: Both readings affirm that genuine reform requires not only zeal but also structure, accountability, and honor given to those who faithfully manage the practical needs of God's people.

study.originalLanguages: The people's response produces an overwhelming maaser, tithe, so abundant that new storerooms must be built to contain it. The chronicler's verdict names Hezekiah's conduct as good, yashar, upright or straight, and true. And his prosperity is tied directly to acting with all his lev, heart, in seeking God, the fullest form of devotion the chronicler can commend.

typology.type: Overflowing tithes and offerings that require new storerooms once true worship is restored
typology.fulfillment: The generous, voluntary sharing of the early church, flowing from genuine encounter with the risen Christ, Acts 2:44-45, 4:32-35.

typology.type: A king who acts with all his heart in seeking God
typology.fulfillment: Christ's own perfect, undivided love for the Father, and the wholehearted love for God He commands of His people, Matthew 22:37.

study.christ: The overwhelming generosity that follows Hezekiah's restored worship, so abundant that new storerooms must be built, foreshadows the pattern of grace producing generosity seen in the New Testament church, where believers held everything in common because of what God had done among them, Acts 4:32. This generosity ultimately flows from the pattern set by Christ Himself, who though He was rich, yet for our sake He became poor, so that we through His poverty might become rich, 2 Corinthians 8:9, the ultimate source of every truly gracious gift.
study.god: God blesses genuine worship and wholehearted seeking with real, tangible abundance, confirmed by the priest Azariah's own testimony that this surplus came because the LORD has blessed His people. God is equally honored by careful, faithful administration of that abundance, delighting not only in generous hearts but in the trustworthy structures that ensure His gifts reach those who serve Him.

crossReferences.note: Test Me in this, says the LORD of Hosts, and see if I will not open the floodgates of heaven, the pattern of blessing following faithful tithing seen here.
The early church's voluntary sharing of possessions, a New Testament echo of the overflowing generosity this chapter records.
God loves a cheerful giver and is able to make all grace abound, so that in every way you may have all you need, the theological principle behind the abundance Hezekiah's people provided.
Love the LORD your God with all your heart, the standard the chronicler explicitly credits to Hezekiah's reign.

study.oneStory: The overflowing tithes and offerings that followed Hezekiah's restoration of worship, so abundant that new storerooms were required, show that genuine devotion to God naturally produces generous, practical care for His house and His servants. Hezekiah's wholehearted seeking of God, the chronicler's highest commendation, points forward to Christ's own perfect, undivided devotion to the Father, and to the grace that, received rightly, always overflows into generosity toward others.

quiz.q: What did the people do immediately after the Passover celebration concluded?

options.text: They went out across Judah and even into Ephraim and Manasseh, destroying every remaining high place, sacred pillar, and Asherah pole.
options.note: Verse 1 records this sweeping, immediate continuation of reform beyond Jerusalem itself.

options.text: They immediately returned home without any further action.
options.note: The text explicitly describes active, ongoing destruction of idolatrous sites across a wide territory before people returned to their own cities.

options.text: They built additional temples to the LORD in every city.
options.note: The described action is destruction of false worship sites, not the construction of additional temples.

options.text: No further action is described after Passover.
options.note: Verse 1 explicitly describes this extensive, immediate follow up action.

quiz.q: How did the people respond once Hezekiah called for contributions to support the priests and Levites?

options.text: They generously brought such an abundant tithe of grain, wine, oil, honey, and livestock that huge heaps accumulated over four months.
options.note: Verses 5 to 7 describe this overwhelming, sustained generosity.

options.text: They refused to contribute anything at all.
options.note: The text explicitly describes overwhelming, generous contribution, the opposite of refusal.

options.text: They contributed only a small, token amount insufficient for the priests' needs.
options.note: The text describes such abundance that new storerooms had to be built and there was plenty left over, not a small or insufficient amount.

options.text: No response to the call for contributions is recorded.
options.note: Verses 5 to 10 give a detailed account of the people's overwhelming response.

quiz.q: What did the priest Azariah tell Hezekiah about the accumulated tithes?

options.text: That since the people began bringing contributions, they had enough to eat with plenty left over, because the LORD had blessed His people.
options.note: Verse 10 records this exact testimony crediting the abundance to God's blessing.

options.text: That the contributions were far too small to meet the priests' basic needs.
options.note: The text explicitly describes abundance and surplus, the opposite of insufficiency.

options.text: That the tithes had been stolen before reaching the temple.
options.note: No theft is mentioned; the report is one of overwhelming abundance safely received and stored.

options.text: No report on the tithes is given to Hezekiah.
options.note: Verse 10 explicitly records Azariah's detailed report.

quiz.q: What administrative structures did Hezekiah establish to manage the abundance of contributions?

options.text: He commanded storerooms to be built and appointed named, trustworthy officials to oversee collection and fair distribution to priests and Levites of all ages.
options.note: Verses 11 to 19 detail this careful administrative system and its named overseers.

options.text: He allowed the contributions to remain unmanaged and unallocated.
options.note: The text describes a detailed, carefully organized system, not an unmanaged accumulation of goods.

options.text: He kept all the contributions for his own personal use.
options.note: The contributions were distributed to priests, Levites, and their families according to need, not retained by the king personally.

options.text: No administrative system is described.
options.note: Verses 11 to 19 give an extensive, detailed account of this administrative structure.

quiz.q: How does the chronicler summarize the reason for Hezekiah's prosperity?

options.text: He did what was good and upright and true before the LORD, acting with all his heart in seeking his God.
options.note: Verses 20 to 21 give this exact summary explicitly tied to his prosperity.

options.text: His prosperity resulted purely from favorable trade agreements with neighboring nations.
options.note: The chronicler attributes his prosperity explicitly to wholehearted devotion to God, not merely favorable political or economic arrangements.

options.text: His prosperity was entirely inherited from his father Ahaz's policies.
options.note: The text explicitly credits Hezekiah's own wholehearted seeking of God, in clear contrast to his father Ahaz's severe unfaithfulness.

options.text: No explanation for his prosperity is given.
options.note: Verses 20 to 21 give an explicit, direct explanation tied to his devotion to God.

questions: The people's generosity overflowed so much that new storerooms had to be built. What would it look like for your own giving, of time, money, or resources, to overflow naturally out of genuine worship rather than being calculated or reluctant?

Hezekiah restored not just dramatic worship moments but the ongoing, practical structures that sustain faithful service over time. What unglamorous, practical habit or structure in your own life needs attention so that your faith is sustained, not just occasionally sparked?

Azariah credited the abundance directly to God's blessing rather than to human effort alone. Where in your own life can you trace back a surplus or provision to God's hand rather than merely your own planning?

The chronicler's highest praise for Hezekiah was that he acted with all his heart in seeking God. Is there an area of your devotion to God that is only partial, done with some but not all of your heart?

Careful, named, accountable officials managed the abundance so it reached those who needed it. Where might God be calling you to serve faithfully in an unseen, administrative way that supports others' ability to worship and serve?

translationNotes.note: The firstfruits offering, alongside the tithe, reflects two related but distinct obligations under the Law, Numbers 18:12 and Leviticus 27:30, both directed toward supporting the priesthood and temple worship.

Males three years of age or older reflects the practice of including even young children of priestly and Levitical families in the distribution rolls, since they too depended on these provisions from birth within their households.
Good and upright and true renders a cluster of Hebrew terms of approval that together form one of the strongest positive evaluations given to any king in the book of Chronicles.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 2 Chronicles 31:1-21. No em dashes or en dashes appeared in the source text for this chapter.
